module_map.responsibility: WSGI/ASGI entry points; loads and chains middleware via BaseHandler; dispatches requests through _get_response/_get_response_async; adapts sync/async methods via asgiref
HTTP request and response abstractions; HttpRequest parses headers, method, body, cookies, POST data; response hierarchy (HttpResponse, StreamingHttpResponse, JsonResponse, etc.); multipart parser
URL routing: pattern matching (regex and path converters), namespace resolution, reverse URL generation; URLResolver walks url_patterns to produce ResolverMatch
View callables and class-based views; error handlers (404/500); debug views; static file serving; i18n views
ORM: model definitions, QuerySet API, SQL compilation, connection management, migrations; database backends abstraction
Request/response processing hooks; CSRF protection, security headers, cache, gzip, locale, CSP, common redirects; all use MiddlewareMixin pattern
Template engine: parsing, rendering, context management, tag/filter libraries; Engine loads backends
Global settings (LazySettings wrapper over global_settings.py); provides settings object imported everywhere
Signal/receiver pub-sub mechanism; Signal class with send/send_robust and connect/disconnect; used for request_started, request_finished, model post_save, etc.
Cross-cutting utilities: encoding, functional helpers (lazy, cached_property), deprecation, log helpers, datastructures, asyncio adapters, decorators
Application registry; AppConfig lifecycle; tracks installed apps and their models
HTML form processing; field validation; widget rendering; ModelForm integration with ORM

pipeline_trace.description: Server (gunicorn/uvicorn) calls WSGIHandler.__call__(environ, start_response) or ASGIHandler.__call__(scope, receive, send). Handler converts raw environ into an HttpRequest and calls get_response.

load_middleware() builds _middleware_chain by wrapping _get_response with each MIDDLEWARE class in reverse order. Each middleware may implement process_request (inbound) and process_response (outbound). Async/sync wrapping done via asgiref sync_to_async/async_to_sync.

BaseHandler.resolve_request() calls get_resolver(urlconf).resolve(request.path_info). URLResolver walks url_patterns, matching RoutePattern or RegexPattern. Returns a ResolverMatch containing the view callable, args, kwargs, url_name, and namespace.

_get_response/_get_response_async calls the resolved callback (function-based or CBV .as_view()) with (request, *args, **kwargs). make_view_atomic wraps the call in a DB transaction if ATOMIC_REQUESTS=True. Middleware process_view hooks fire before invocation.

View code calls QuerySet methods (filter, get, annotate, select_related). QuerySet.iterator() triggers SQL compilation via SQLCompiler, which hands off to a DatabaseWrapper backend. Results are returned as Model instances.
View calls render() or returns TemplateResponse. Template.render(context, request) evaluates nodes with a RequestContext populated from context_processors. Output is a unicode string.
View returns an HttpResponse (or subclass). Middleware process_response hooks fire in reverse order, then the response is serialized back to the server by WSGIHandler or ASGIHandler.

cross_module_hubs.reason: settings is imported by virtually every module in the codebase (middleware, ORM backends, URL resolver, template engine, cache, etc.) to read configuration. It has no upstream Django deps, making it a pure sink hub with maximum fan-in.

ImproperlyConfigured, Http404, PermissionDenied, ValidationError, and MiddlewareNotUsed are raised throughout every layer (ORM, forms, middleware, URL routing, views). It depends only on builtins, so it acts as a central exception vocabulary shared across all modules.

Provides lazy(), cached_property, SimpleLazyObject, and keep_lazy which are foundational primitives used by conf.LazySettings, ORM fields, template context, http.request, and many others. High fan-in from nearly every package; low outbound coupling.

change_proposal.feature_summary: Add an async-compatible structured logging middleware that captures per-request metadata (view name, ORM query count, response time) and emits a structured log entry at request completion.
files_to_modify: django/utils/log.py (optionally extend log formatters or add helper)
new_types: StructuredLogMiddleware(MiddlewareMixin) — implements __init__(get_response), process_request (record start time), process_response (compute elapsed, read db query count, emit log record), and async variants via MiddlewareMixin's adapt_method_mode support
StructuredLogRecord(TypedDict) — { 'view': str, 'method': str, 'path': str, 'status': int, 'queries': int, 'duration_ms': float, 'request_id': str | None }
change_proposal.pattern_to_follow: Follow django/middleware/common.py: extend MiddlewareMixin (from django.utils.deprecation), implement process_request and process_response hooks. For async compatibility, MiddlewareMixin already handles sync/async bridging via asgiref when is_coroutine is detected, so no manual async_to_sync wrapping is needed if both sync and async hooks are provided. Use django.db import connections to read len(connections['default'].queries) when DEBUG=True (or django.test.utils.CaptureQueriesContext pattern for production via a custom connection wrapper).
change_proposal.integration_point: Add 'django.middleware.structured_log.StructuredLogMiddleware' to MIDDLEWARE in settings after SecurityMiddleware but before CommonMiddleware so it wraps the full view pipeline. process_request records time.monotonic() start on request. process_response computes elapsed, reads query count from connection.queries_log or django.db.reset_queries instrumentation, then calls logging.getLogger('django.request').info() with the StructuredLogRecord as the extra= dict.
risks: ORM query count requires DEBUG=True or a custom DatabaseWrapper instrumentation; production deployments with DEBUG=False will see queries=0 unless django.db.backends signals (pre_execute/post_execute via connection.execute_wrapper) are used, adding per-query overhead.

Attaching state to the request object (start_time, query_count) is not thread-safe for streaming responses or early-exit paths where process_response may not be called; must guard with hasattr checks.

async process_response must not call sync ORM or I/O; emitting logs via logging.getLogger is safe since the standard logging handlers are sync — async views will run process_response in the async event loop which may block if a slow log handler (e.g. SocketHandler) is configured.
Middleware ordering matters: placing after CsrfViewMiddleware means CSRF-rejected requests are still logged; placing before means the view name may be None if the resolver short-circuits early.

Adding per-request log output increases I/O pressure under high traffic; should be guarded by a configurable log level (e.g. INFO by default, disabled by setting logger level to WARNING in production).
